cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure out the answer here. The question is asking why a novelist can expect the publisher to print the manuscript exactly as written. The options are A, B, C, or D. First, I need to refer back to the passage provided. The passage starts by talking about novels versus plays and screenplays. It mentions that novelists have absolute control over their text until it's published. The key sentence here is: "The novelist keeps absolute control over his text until it is published and received by the audience." It also says that even if the writer refuses to revise based on an editor's advice, people wouldn't be surprised. And there's an example that a well-established novelist might submit a manuscript and expect it to be printed as is. So the main point here is about the control the novelist has. The question is why they can expect the manuscript to be printed exactly as written. Let's look at the options again. Option A: Because the novelist keeps absolute control over his text. That seems to directly quote from the passage. The passage says the novelist has absolute control until publication. So if that's the case, then they can expect it to be printed as is because they have that control. Option B: Because the paperback is portable
